title: Comprehensive Smoking Cessation Pharmacotherapy + Health Promotion Visit for 58-Year-Old Man with COPD GOLD Stage 3 + 35 Pack-Year Smoking History After Recent ED Visit for Exacerbation: Varenicline vs Bupropion vs Combination Nicotine Replacement Selection, 5 As Framework + Behavioral Counseling, ACIP Adult Vaccination Catch-Up, Annual Low-Dose CT Lung Cancer Screening, Pulmonary Rehabilitation Referral, and Cardiovascular Risk Reduction
subtitle: TOBACCO USE DISORDER is the LEADING preventable cause of death in US (~480,000 deaths/year; ~1 in 5 adult deaths) + major modifiable risk factor for COPD + lung cancer + cardiovascular disease + cerebrovascular disease + multiple cancers + many other conditions; SMOKING CESSATION at any age provides health benefits — within 20 min HR/BP normalize, 12 hr CO levels normalize, 2 weeks-3 months circulation/lung function improve, 1 year coronary heart disease risk halves, 5 years stroke risk drops to non-smoker level + multiple cancer risks decline, 10 years lung cancer death rate halves vs continued smoking, 15 years CHD risk equals non-smoker; 5 AS BRIEF INTERVENTION FRAMEWORK by USPHS clinical practice guideline (every visit, every smoker): ASK about tobacco use; ADVISE to quit (clear, strong, personalized); ASSESS readiness to quit (precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance per Transtheoretical/Stages of Change Model); ASSIST in quit attempt (counseling + pharmacotherapy + Quitline + apps); ARRANGE follow-up (within 1 week of quit date); SMOKING CESSATION PHARMACOTHERAPY (first-line agents with substantial evidence): (1) VARENICLINE (Chantix) — partial nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist (α4β2 subtype); MOST EFFECTIVE single agent + highest quit rates (~25-30% at 1 yr vs ~10-15% placebo); titration: 0.5 mg PO daily × 3 days → 0.5 mg PO BID × 4 days → 1 mg PO BID × 11 weeks (typical 12-week course); SET QUIT DATE 1-2 weeks after starting; alternative gradual reduction over 12 weeks while smoking; ADVERSE EFFECTS: nausea (most common 30%; take with food + water + smaller dose initial), vivid/abnormal dreams, insomnia, headache, constipation; PSYCHIATRIC concerns previously raised black-box (removed 2016 after EAGLES trial showed no excess risk of serious neuropsychiatric events in stable psychiatric patients but maintain caution + screening); EAGLES trial 2016 confirmed superior efficacy + similar safety to placebo for serious neuropsychiatric AEs in both psych + non-psych patients; renal dose adjustment if CrCl <30 — reduce to 0.5 mg daily then BID; cardiovascular events possible (slight ↑ vs placebo per some trials but absolute risk low; weigh benefits in CV patients); use cautiously in seizure disorder; (2) BUPROPION SR (Zyban / Wellbutrin SR) — atypical antidepressant + dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; mechanism unclear but reduces nicotine withdrawal + cravings; 150 mg PO daily × 3 days → 150 mg PO BID × 7-12 weeks; SET QUIT DATE 1 week after starting; ADVERSE EFFECTS: insomnia (most common), dry mouth, headache, tremor, nausea; CONTRAINDICATIONS: SEIZURE DISORDER (LOWERS SEIZURE THRESHOLD — absolute contraindication), eating disorder (anorexia/bulimia — absolute), MAOI use within 14 days, abrupt alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal, history of recent head trauma; black-box warning for SUICIDAL IDEATION in patients <25 yr though less prominent than SSRIs; avoid with concurrent significant alcohol intake; can lower seizure threshold; (3) NICOTINE REPLACEMENT THERAPY (NRT) — addresses nicotine withdrawal without harmful combustion products; multiple formulations + COMBINATION more effective than monotherapy; LONG-ACTING = transdermal patch (steady level over 24 hr; sleep disturbance may need 16-hr patch); SHORT-ACTING = gum (chewed slowly + parked between cheek + gum for absorption; not chewed continuously) + lozenge + inhaler + nasal spray; CARDIOVASCULAR concerns previously raised but EVIDENCE shows NRT safer than continued smoking in CV disease + acceptable in stable CV disease; pregnancy use cautiously + behavioral first-line; dosing based on smoking intensity (typically patch 21 mg/day if >10 cigarettes/day for 4-6 weeks then taper); COMBINATION patch + short-acting more effective than either alone — patch for baseline + gum/lozenge for breakthrough cravings; COMBINATION THERAPY: (1) Varenicline + NRT — evidence supports for heavy smokers + difficulty quitting (not contraindicated; safer than smoking); (2) Bupropion + NRT — synergistic + acceptable; (3) PATCH + SHORT-ACTING NRT — combination most effective NRT; AVOID Bupropion + Varenicline combination (CHRONICUS trial); CYTISINICLINE recently FDA-approved (2024) alternative; AVOID e-cigarettes for cessation per current evidence (limited data + concerns); ANNUAL LDCT LUNG CANCER SCREENING per USPSTF 2021: age 50-80 + 20+ pack-year smoking history + currently smoke or quit within 15 yr — reduces lung cancer mortality ~20%; ACIP VACCINATIONS for adult with COPD: PCV20 once OR PCV15 followed by PPSV23 ≥1 yr later (PCV15 → PPSV23 acceptable; PCV20 simpler one-time; pneumococcal vaccination for COPD per ACIP); INFLUENZA annually; COVID-19 per current guidance; Tdap booster every 10 yr (last Tdap 8 yr ago — due in 2 yr but can give now); SHINGRIX 2-dose series age ≥50 (this patient eligible); HEPATITIS B catch-up; PULMONARY REHAB referral per evidence (improves QOL + exercise capacity + reduces hospitalizations + Class A recommendation); CV RISK reduction (statin + ACE-I if indicated + BP/lipid/glucose screen + lifestyle); SLEEP APNEA screening (high prevalence in COPD + cardiovascular risk); MENTAL HEALTH screening (depression + anxiety common with chronic illness + nicotine cessation)

labs.v: 58 y/o male D. Hayes, retired construction foreman × 3 years, presents for outpatient PCP follow-up after recent ED visit + 3-day hospitalization for COPD exacerbation 2 weeks ago. PMH: COPD GOLD Stage 3 (severe, FEV1 35% predicted on most recent PFT 8 months ago) diagnosed 8 yr ago + chronic + multiple exacerbations (3 in past year — current ED visit is 3rd); HTN (lisinopril 10 mg daily — controlled), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 40 mg daily); pre-diabetes (HbA1c 6.0 — borderline); chronic low back pain (occasional acetaminophen + nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs as needed); no other chronic diseases. SOCIAL: married 30 years to wife (54, retired nurse — supportive + non-smoker + advocate for quitting); 2 adult children (32, 28, both living with families nearby + non-smokers); 7-year-old granddaughter (close relationship — visits weekly + identified as motivator for quitting); retired from construction industry × 3 years (occupational exposure to dust + asbestos + silica + diesel exhaust + secondhand smoke); long-time community member + active in local church + close friends + supportive social network. SMOKING HISTORY: began smoking at age 17 (peer + family + 'cool factor' in 1985); chronic smoker × 41 years; current consumption 25-30 cig/day × past 5 years; PACK-YEAR CALCULATION: 25 cig/day × 41 yr ÷ 20 cig/pack = ~51 pack-years (re-calculated by FNP — patient reported 35 pack-years but actual closer to 50); first cigarette within 30 min of waking + 'usually first thing' = HIGH nicotine dependence per Fagerström Test for Nicotine Dependence; ATTEMPTS to quit: 3 attempts × past 10 years (without pharmacotherapy or formal behavioral support — relapsed within 2 weeks each time despite genuine intent); never tried prescription pharmacotherapy + briefly tried NRT patch unsuccessfully (likely under-dosed for heavy smoking); BARRIERS to quitting historically: nicotine cravings + withdrawal symptoms + weight gain (concerned about already-overweight + low metabolism) + social factors (some friends still smoke) + occupational stress (formerly) + habit/oral fixation + irritability + difficulty concentrating + interpersonal conflict with family if irritable; MOTIVATORS for quitting NOW: (1) recent COPD exacerbation + ED + hospitalization (acute health scare); (2) progressive symptoms + dyspnea + cough + functional limitation; (3) GRANDDAUGHTER (7-yr-old) asking about cough; (4) wedding of granddaughter (future event in 2 years); (5) wife's advocacy; (6) FNP recommendation (medical home relationship × 8 years); (7) financial cost of cigarettes ($300+/month); (8) cleaner home environment for grandchildren visits; (9) reduce risk of stroke + lung cancer + heart attack. READINESS TO QUIT per Transtheoretical/Stages of Change Model: PREPARATION (planning to quit within 30 days + has tried before + serious intent); seeking pharmacotherapy support + behavioral support + Quitline + community; quit date being set today at FNP visit. CURRENT MEDICATIONS: lisinopril 10 mg PO daily + atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily + tiotropium 18 mcg DPI daily (long-acting muscarinic antagonist LAMA) + fluticasone/salmeterol 250/50 DPI BID (ICS/LABA combination for moderate-severe COPD per GOLD ABCD per high exacerbation history) + albuterol HFA MDI 2 puffs q4-6h PRN (SABA rescue) + finishing prednisone 40 mg taper Day 12 of 15 (40 mg × 5 days → 20 mg × 5 days → 10 mg × 5 days for COPD exacerbation) + azithromycin 500 mg PO daily Day 5 of 5 (course completing today). VITAL SIGNS: HR 82, BP 138/86, RR 16, SpO2 94% RA (chronic hypoxemia + baseline ~93-95%), T 36.7°C, weight 78 kg (BMI 26.9), waist circumference 102 cm (abdominal obesity). EXAM: appropriate + cooperative + slight tobacco odor + finger clubbing mild + carboxyhemoglobin 4% (elevated for smoker — would be 0-1% non-smoker); chest = bilateral coarse crackles + scattered wheezes + slightly diminished breath sounds + accessory muscle use mild + chronic + no acute distress; cardiac S1/S2 normal + no murmurs; abdomen soft + obese; extremities + neuro intact. LABS recent (within past 3 months — recent ED visit): CBC normal; CMP normal; HbA1c 6.0; lipid panel — LDL 102 (on statin), HDL 38 (low — common in smokers), triglycerides 168, total cholesterol 178; BNP 95 (normal — no CHF); troponin negative; chest X-ray = chronic COPD changes + hyperinflation + no acute infiltrate or mass; PFT 8 months ago = FEV1 35% predicted (severe COPD GOLD Stage 3), FEV1/FVC 0.55 (obstructive pattern); ECG normal sinus rhythm + no acute changes; CT chest 4 yr ago for cough workup showed emphysema + no lung mass — LDCT screening due. MENTAL HEALTH: PHQ-9 4 (minimal depression — recovered from previous mild depression on sertraline 6 yr ago + discontinued); GAD-7 6 (mild anxiety — chronic illness + functional decline); no current substance abuse beyond tobacco; no alcohol abuse (occasional 1-2 drinks/week + glass of wine with dinner); no illicit drug use; no history of suicidal ideation or self-harm; supportive family + community.
labs.r: TOBACCO USE DISORDER + COPD GOLD Stage 3 + cardiovascular risk + multimorbidity = high-yield opportunity for comprehensive Health Promotion intervention. SMOKING HISTORY thorough — pack-years + cessation attempts + barriers + motivators + readiness essential for tailored intervention. RECOGNIZING readiness to quit + assessing dependence + co-morbidities + selecting pharmacotherapy + behavioral support + comprehensive Health Promotion = standard adult outpatient care.

labs.n: SMOKING CESSATION 5 As + Transtheoretical Model + Behavioral Counseling
labs.v: 5 AS BRIEF INTERVENTION FRAMEWORK per US Public Health Service (USPHS) Clinical Practice Guideline for Treating Tobacco Use and Dependence — recommended at EVERY visit, EVERY smoker: (1) ASK about tobacco use — implement system to identify ALL tobacco users at every visit + document in chart; (2) ADVISE to quit — clear, strong, personalized cessation message: 'Quitting smoking is the most important thing you can do for your health'; 'Your COPD will progress without quitting'; 'I urge you to quit'; (3) ASSESS readiness to quit — willing in next 30 days = ready; if NOT ready, brief motivational intervention (5 Rs: Relevance + Risks + Rewards + Roadblocks + Repetition); (4) ASSIST in quit attempt — set quit date within 2 weeks (this patient = 1 week today); offer pharmacotherapy + behavioral counseling + Quitline + apps + community resources; provide written cessation plan; address barriers + social support; anticipatory guidance for withdrawal; relapse prevention; (5) ARRANGE follow-up — within 1 week of quit date (most important time for relapse) + 1 month + 3 months + ongoing; many quitters require 5-7 attempts before sustained cessation. TRANSTHEORETICAL MODEL OF CHANGE / STAGES OF CHANGE per Prochaska + DiClemente: (1) PRECONTEMPLATION — not considering change in next 6 months (~40% smokers); (2) CONTEMPLATION — considering change in next 6 months (~40%); (3) PREPARATION — planning change in next 30 days + may have tried before (~20% — this patient); (4) ACTION — actively making changes (in first 6 months); (5) MAINTENANCE — sustaining change >6 months; RELAPSE possible at any stage + part of process (normalize without judgment). MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING techniques for not-yet-ready or ambivalent smokers: (1) EXPRESS empathy; (2) DEVELOP discrepancy (between current behavior + goals/values); (3) AVOID argumentation; (4) ROLL with resistance; (5) SUPPORT self-efficacy; OARS = Open-ended questions + Affirmations + Reflective listening + Summarizing; AVOID lecturing + scare tactics alone (rarely effective); EMPHASIZE patient autonomy. BEHAVIORAL COUNSELING components: identify trigger situations + craving management (5 D's = Delay, Deep breathing, Distract, Drink water, Discuss) + alternative behaviors + social support + reward system + handling slips (one cigarette doesn't mean failure — re-commit) + relapse prevention strategies; INTENSITY: Brief (3-10 min) vs Intensive (4+ sessions, 30+ min) — INTENSIVE more effective; INDIVIDUAL or GROUP both effective. QUITLINE 1-800-QUIT-NOW (1-800-784-8669) = state-based + free + multi-language + counseling + materials + sometimes free NRT; effective + accessible. APPS (SmokefreeTXT + QuitSTART + others) + online resources (CDC Tips From Former Smokers + WhyQuit + smokefree.gov). COMMUNITY support groups (American Lung Association Freedom From Smoking + Nicotine Anonymous + faith-based) + peer support. HEALTH SYSTEM SUPPORT — tobacco-free workplaces + insurance coverage + medical home + EHR documentation + quality measures.
labs.r: 5 AS + STAGES OF CHANGE + MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING + BEHAVIORAL COUNSELING + QUITLINE + APPS + COMMUNITY = comprehensive smoking cessation behavioral framework. Foundational to clinical practice. RN role: routinely assess + advise + assist + arrange follow-up + provide resources + emotional support.

labs.n: SMOKING CESSATION PHARMACOTHERAPY — Varenicline vs Bupropion vs NRT
labs.v: SMOKING CESSATION PHARMACOTHERAPY first-line agents per USPHS clinical practice guideline + Cochrane reviews + meta-analyses: (1) VARENICLINE (Chantix) — partial α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist; MOST EFFECTIVE single agent + highest quit rates (~25-30% at 1 yr vs ~10-15% placebo); MECHANISM = partial agonist reduces nicotine withdrawal + cravings (agonist activity) + blocks reward of smoking if patient slips (antagonist activity); TITRATION: 0.5 mg PO daily × 3 days → 0.5 mg PO BID × 4 days → 1 mg PO BID × 11 weeks total 12-week course; SET QUIT DATE 1-2 weeks AFTER starting (gradual decrease in nicotine cravings + brain receptor adaptation; gradually reduce smoking in pre-quit phase if difficult to stop abruptly); ALTERNATIVE strategies: gradual reduction approach (reduce 50% by week 4, 75% by week 8, stop by week 12 — for patients struggling); CONTINUE for 12 weeks total + can extend to 24 weeks for high-risk relapse patients (per maintenance trials); ADVERSE EFFECTS: NAUSEA (most common ~30% — take with food + 8 oz water + smaller initial doses if intolerable); VIVID/ABNORMAL DREAMS (especially during titration; may resolve with continued use); INSOMNIA (take last dose with dinner not bedtime); HEADACHE; CONSTIPATION; PSYCHIATRIC concerns: previous black-box warning (depression + agitation + suicidality) REMOVED in 2016 after EAGLES trial — 8000+ patients including 4000 with stable psychiatric disease showed NO EXCESS of serious neuropsychiatric AEs vs placebo or NRT or bupropion; superior efficacy vs others; SOME RISK of mood changes in patients with active or recent severe psychiatric disease — screen + monitor; CARDIOVASCULAR concerns: meta-analysis suggested slight increase in MACE (major adverse cardiovascular events) vs placebo but absolute risk low + benefits of cessation typically outweigh; use cautiously in recent (within 30 days) MI or stroke or unstable angina; RENAL dose adjustment for CrCl <30 (0.5 mg daily → 0.5 mg BID); cautious use in seizure disorder; FDA-APPROVED for adults 18+. (2) BUPROPION SR (Zyban / Wellbutrin SR) — atypical antidepressant + dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; mechanism in smoking cessation incompletely understood but reduces withdrawal + cravings + addresses concurrent depression; QUIT RATES ~15-20% at 1 yr vs ~10% placebo (less effective than varenicline but more than placebo); DOSING: 150 mg PO daily × 3 days → 150 mg PO BID × 7-12 weeks; SET QUIT DATE 1 week after starting (faster ramp); CONTRAINDICATIONS: SEIZURE DISORDER (absolute — LOWERS SEIZURE THRESHOLD); EATING DISORDER (anorexia/bulimia — absolute; electrolyte concerns + seizure threshold); MAOI use within 14 days; abrupt alcohol or benzodiazepine WITHDRAWAL (lowers seizure threshold); RECENT HEAD TRAUMA (relative); SEVERE hepatic impairment; pediatric. ADVERSE EFFECTS: INSOMNIA (most common — take second dose 8 hr after first + before 5 PM); DRY MOUTH; HEADACHE; TREMOR; NAUSEA; agitation; rash; constipation. BLACK-BOX warning for SUICIDAL IDEATION in <25 yr (less prominent than SSRIs but present). FOR PATIENTS WITH CONCURRENT DEPRESSION: bupropion treats both. WEIGHT NEUTRAL or slight loss (vs SSRI weight gain). (3) NICOTINE REPLACEMENT THERAPY (NRT) — provides nicotine without harmful combustion products (carbon monoxide + tar + 7000+ chemicals + 70+ carcinogens); reduces withdrawal symptoms while user works on behavioral change; FORMULATIONS: LONG-ACTING = PATCH 21 mg/day if >10 cig/day or 14 mg/day if 5-10 cig/day or 7 mg/day for <5 cig/day; apply daily × 4-6 weeks then taper to lower dose; rotate sites; can use 24-hr or 16-hr (off at bedtime for sleep disturbance); SHORT-ACTING = GUM (chewed + parked between cheek + gum for absorption, NOT continuous chewing) 2 mg if <25 cig/day or 4 mg if ≥25 cig/day q1-2h up to 24/day × 6 weeks then taper; LOZENGE (held in mouth + dissolve slowly + alternate sides) 2 mg or 4 mg similarly; INHALER (puff like cigarette); NASAL SPRAY (rapid; reserved for refractory). DOSING based on smoking intensity (this patient 25-30 cig/day = patch 21 mg/day + gum 4 mg). COMBINATION patch + short-acting NRT MORE EFFECTIVE than monotherapy for heavy smokers (patch baseline + short-acting for cravings). CV concerns: previously raised but EVIDENCE shows NRT safer than continued smoking in CV disease + acceptable in stable CV disease; pregnancy use cautiously + behavioral first-line; recent MI/stroke (<2 weeks) caution; arrhythmia caution. ADVERSE EFFECTS: skin irritation (patch — rotate sites + topical hydrocortisone if needed); mouth/throat irritation (gum/lozenge — slow down chewing/dissolving); jaw soreness (gum — limit); insomnia (24-hr patch — switch to 16-hr or remove at bedtime); GI upset; headache. AVAILABLE OTC (patch + gum + lozenge) + Rx (inhaler + nasal spray + selected formulations). COMBINATION THERAPY: (1) VARENICLINE + NRT — evidence supports for heavy smokers + previous unsuccessful attempts; not contraindicated; safer than continued smoking; (2) BUPROPION + NRT — synergistic + acceptable + addresses concurrent depression; (3) PATCH + SHORT-ACTING NRT = standard combination NRT; AVOID BUPROPION + VARENICLINE combination (CHRONICUS trial showed no benefit + increased adverse effects). 2ND-LINE AGENTS: clonidine (off-label) + nortriptyline (off-label). RECENT FDA-APPROVED: CYTISINICLINE (2024) — partial nicotinic receptor agonist similar to varenicline; well-tolerated + emerging alternative. AVOID e-cigarettes (vaping) for cessation per current evidence — limited data + concerns about vaping-related lung injury (EVALI) + nicotine addiction perpetuation + youth uptake + insufficient long-term safety data; some experts argue harm reduction role but mainstream guidelines do NOT endorse + recommend established pharmacotherapy.
labs.r: VARENICLINE most effective single agent; BUPROPION + NRT effective alternatives; COMBINATION therapy more effective for heavy smokers. ADVERSE EFFECTS + CONTRAINDICATIONS + CARDIOVASCULAR + PSYCHIATRIC considerations integral. EAGLES trial 2016 removed varenicline black-box. RN role: select per patient profile + counsel + monitor + adjust + manage side effects + support adherence.

labs.n: VARENICLINE selection rationale + monitoring + dosing for THIS PATIENT
labs.v: VARENICLINE selected for THIS PATIENT after shared decision-making + assessment: ADVANTAGES = (1) MOST EFFECTIVE single agent (1-yr quit rate ~25-30% vs ~10-15% placebo); patient has 3 prior failed attempts without pharmacotherapy + likely benefits most from highest-efficacy agent; (2) heavy smoker (25-30 cig/day) + high nicotine dependence (first cigarette within 30 min of waking = Fagerström high score) + would benefit from full nicotine receptor support; (3) no seizure disorder + no eating disorder + no MAOI use = no contraindications; (4) no recent MI/stroke + no unstable angina + stable cardiovascular disease on lisinopril/atorvastatin = CV considerations acceptable with monitoring; (5) PHQ-9 4 + GAD-7 6 = no active severe psychiatric disease (prior mild depression resolved); (6) renal function normal (Cr 0.9); (7) ADHERENT to medication regimens historically; (8) MOTIVATED + supportive wife + family. DISADVANTAGES discussed: (1) nausea common — take with food + 8 oz water + tolerance often improves; (2) vivid dreams may occur (some find unsettling but typically self-limit); (3) insomnia — take second dose with dinner not bedtime; (4) need for prescription + insurance coverage (patient has insurance covering varenicline); (5) cost (~$300-400/month brand; generic ~$100-200/month; insurance copay varies; manufacturer patient assistance available). DOSING for THIS PATIENT (12-week course): WEEK 1 = varenicline 0.5 mg PO daily × Days 1-3, then 0.5 mg PO BID × Days 4-7; WEEK 2-12 = varenicline 1 mg PO BID; SET QUIT DATE Day 7 (after first full week of dose-adjustment to 0.5 mg BID); pre-quit smoking allowed + actively reducing in first week; quit cold turkey Day 7 OR gradual reduction approach (50% reduction by week 4, 75% by week 8, stop by week 12) for patients struggling with cold turkey. PATIENT EDUCATION at first dose: (1) PURPOSE = reduce cravings + withdrawal + block reward of smoking if slip occurs; (2) BENEFITS + EFFICACY data + realistic expectations (not magic — still need behavioral counseling + commitment); (3) ADMINISTRATION = with food + 8 oz water + same time each day + don't skip; (4) SIDE EFFECTS — nausea (most common) + vivid dreams + insomnia + report severe; (5) PSYCHIATRIC monitoring — report mood changes + agitation + worsening depression + suicidal thoughts; (6) STORAGE + safety; (7) DURATION = 12 weeks total + can extend to 24 weeks if needed; (8) BEHAVIORAL counseling + Quitline + community essential; (9) FOLLOW-UP critical at 1 week + 1 month + 3 months. CONCURRENT recommendations: continue COPD inhalers + medications; behavioral counseling + Quitline 1-800-QUIT-NOW + smartphone app + community support group (American Lung Association Freedom From Smoking); weight management + exercise + diet (anticipated 5-10 lb weight gain after quitting — minimal vs benefits + manageable); stress management + alternative behaviors for oral fixation (sugar-free gum + water + carrots/celery + deep breathing). DOCUMENTATION of shared decision-making + informed consent + risks/benefits discussion + treatment plan + follow-up.
labs.r: VARENICLINE selected for high efficacy + heavy smoker + previous failed attempts + no contraindications. Dosing titration + quit date + patient education + behavioral counseling + community support + follow-up = comprehensive plan. RN role: medication education + adherence + monitoring + support + escalation if side effects + follow-up.

labs.n: ACIP VACCINATIONS for adult with COPD + age 58 — comprehensive update
labs.v: ACIP-RECOMMENDED VACCINATIONS for adult age 58 with COPD per current ACIP guidelines: (1) INFLUENZA annually for all 6 mo+ during influenza season — high priority in COPD (reduces exacerbations + hospitalizations + mortality); quadrivalent or high-dose for 65+ (this patient 58 — standard quadrivalent acceptable); current administration TODAY in clinic; (2) PNEUMOCOCCAL — adults with chronic lung disease (including COPD) receive pneumococcal vaccination per ACIP; current OPTIONS: (a) PCV20 (Prevnar 20) once — preferred for simplicity (covers 20 serotypes including those in PCV13 + PPSV23 overlap); (b) PCV15 (Vaxneuvance) + PPSV23 (Pneumovax 23) ≥1 yr later — alternative; (c) for previously vaccinated patients per ACIP updated tables; THIS PATIENT receives PCV20 today in clinic (preferred simpler approach); follow-up reminder for any future ACIP changes; (3) TDAP every 10 yr for tetanus + diphtheria + pertussis booster; this patient last Tdap 8 yr ago — administered Tdap TODAY (preference to administer Tdap when seeing patient + cover pertussis transmission especially around grandchildren); (4) SHINGRIX (recombinant zoster vaccine) — 2-DOSE series 2-6 months apart for adults age ≥50; significantly reduces shingles + post-herpetic neuralgia; this patient age 58 ELIGIBLE — schedule dose 1 next visit (today's visit busy with cessation + influenza + PCV20 + Tdap = 3 vaccines; defer Shingrix to next visit to avoid injection burden + tolerability); (5) COVID-19 per current CDC guidance — updated annual booster if eligible; this patient received recent booster 3 months ago + up to date; (6) HEPATITIS B catch-up — universal recommendation expanded to all adults age 19-59 since 2022; this patient eligible — schedule 3-dose series next visits (HBV-Recombivax 0/1/6 month or HepB-Engerix or HepB-Heplisav-B 2-dose); (7) HEPATITIS A consider per occupational/travel/other risk; (8) MMR + VARICELLA + ROTAVIRUS — series typically complete in childhood; verify; (9) HPV — recommended through age 26 + shared clinical decision-making 27-45 (this patient 58 — older than typical recommendation but consider in some cases). DOCUMENTATION + CDC immunization registry (CAIR2 in California) + state IIS reporting. PATIENT EDUCATION at administration: VIS (Vaccine Information Statement) per CDC + observation 15 min post-vaccination for syncope/reaction + post-vaccination care + signs to report. CONTRAINDICATIONS: severe allergy to vaccine component (specific to product); moderate-severe acute illness (defer); pregnancy considerations + immunocompromise considerations + live vs non-live (most adult vaccines non-live); this patient no contraindications.
labs.r: ACIP VACCINATIONS for adult with COPD comprehensive — influenza annually + pneumococcal PCV20 + Tdap + Shingrix + COVID-19 + HBV catch-up. Standard adult Health Promotion + immunization update. RN role: review + administer + counsel + VIS + observation + documentation + registry reporting.

labs.n: LDCT LUNG CANCER SCREENING + USPSTF criteria + shared decision-making
labs.v: ANNUAL LDCT (low-dose computed tomography) LUNG CANCER SCREENING per USPSTF 2021 (updated from 2013) Grade B recommendation: ELIGIBILITY = age 50-80 + 20+ pack-year smoking history + currently smoke or quit within 15 years; EVIDENCE = National Lung Screening Trial (NLST) showed ~20% reduction in lung cancer mortality + 6.7% reduction in all-cause mortality vs chest X-ray screening in high-risk smokers; NELSON trial 2020 confirmed mortality benefit. THIS PATIENT eligibility met: age 58 + ~51 pack-years (recalculated by FNP — patient reported 35 pack-years but actual closer to 50) + currently smoking; ALL CRITERIA MET. SHARED DECISION-MAKING per USPSTF + CMS: (1) BENEFITS — ~20% lung cancer mortality reduction; early detection + curative treatment (Stage 1 lung cancer ~80-90% 5-yr survival vs ~15% Stage 4); (2) RISKS — false positive ~26% (most resolve with follow-up + don't require biopsy); incidental findings + workup (some benign + some concerning); RADIATION exposure ~1.5 mSv per scan (low-dose) + cumulative over years; overdiagnosis (treatment of non-progressive cancers — uncertain magnitude); biopsy risks + false reassurance; (3) IMPORTANCE of continued cessation (LDCT does NOT replace cessation; cessation reduces lung cancer + ASCVD risk most); (4) FREQUENCY = annual screening until age 80 OR 15 years post-cessation OR develops health problem precluding curative treatment; (5) Lung-RADS reporting system standardizes results + management; (6) covered by Medicare + most insurance + ACA preventive coverage. DOCUMENT shared decision-making conversation + benefits + risks + patient understanding + decision; ORDER LDCT chest non-contrast per institutional protocol; REFER to radiology + tracking system for annual reminders. AVOID screening in: limited life expectancy + significant comorbidities + unable to tolerate workup + tobacco use disorder not motivated for cessation (controversial — some argue screening should be paired with cessation intervention).
labs.r: LDCT LUNG CANCER SCREENING per USPSTF 2021 + age 50-80 + 20+ pack-years + current smoker or quit within 15 yr. Grade B recommendation. Shared decision-making essential. RN role: identify eligibility + counsel + order + track + ensure follow-up + reinforce cessation.

labs.n: COPD comprehensive management + pulmonary rehab + cardiovascular risk reduction
labs.v: COPD GOLD GUIDELINE-BASED MANAGEMENT for THIS PATIENT (GOLD Stage 3 severe + multiple exacerbations + history of hospitalization): (1) PHARMACOTHERAPY per GOLD ABCD: this patient = GOLD D (severe + ≥2 exacerbations or 1 hospitalization) → LAMA + LABA + ICS triple therapy (currently on tiotropium + fluticasone/salmeterol); SABA rescue + consider PHOSPHODIESTERASE-4 INHIBITOR (roflumilast) for chronic bronchitis + severe COPD with frequent exacerbations; AZITHROMYCIN 250 mg PO 3x/week long-term for frequent exacerbations (this patient currently completing acute course — discuss long-term maintenance with ID + pulmonology); MUC INHIBITORS + N-ACETYLCYSTEINE for chronic bronchitis some evidence; ANTI-IL5 (mepolizumab + reslizumab + benralizumab) for severe eosinophilic COPD; OXYGEN therapy if PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88% (continuous improves survival); NIPPV (BIPAP) at night for hypercapnic respiratory failure (vs ARDS use); (2) PULMONARY REHABILITATION — evidence-based + Class A recommendation; 8-12 week multidisciplinary program with exercise training + education + nutrition + psychosocial support + smoking cessation reinforcement + breathing techniques + energy conservation + medication management + flare-up management; IMPROVES QOL + exercise capacity + dyspnea + reduces hospitalizations + healthcare utilization; insurance coverage variable but Medicare covers post-exacerbation pulmonary rehab; THIS PATIENT referred today; (3) SMOKING CESSATION = most important intervention (single greatest impact on COPD progression + mortality); (4) VACCINATIONS as above; (5) NUTRITION counseling (~25% of COPD patients underweight + ~30% overweight; cachexia in advanced; obesity also problematic — Mediterranean diet + adequate protein + supplementation if cachexia); (6) MENTAL HEALTH screen + treat depression/anxiety common in COPD; (7) SLEEP screening for OSA (high co-prevalence + cardiovascular risk + worsens hypoxemia); (8) PALLIATIVE CARE integration earlier in advanced COPD (symptoms + advance care planning + hospice eligibility per criteria); (9) HOSPITALIZATION prevention bundle (post-discharge transition of care + medication reconciliation + adherence + follow-up + action plan + smoking cessation + vaccination + pulmonary rehab + secondary prevention). CARDIOVASCULAR RISK REDUCTION for adult smoker with COPD + HTN + lipid abnormality: (1) ASCVD calculator (10-yr risk per ACC/AHA pooled cohort) — this patient elevated due to age + smoking + HTN + lipids — ASCVD risk likely 15-25% (high); (2) STATIN intensity per ACC/AHA — atorvastatin 40 mg appropriate (high-intensity) for high-risk patient; LDL target <70 in high-risk; this patient LDL 102 on statin — consider intensification (atorvastatin 80 mg) or ezetimibe addition or PCSK9 inhibitor; (3) BP management — lisinopril 10 mg + relaxed targets per JNC/AHA in elderly + cardiovascular disease + COPD (SBP <130 ideal but balance with orthostasis + adherence); (4) ASPIRIN — primary prevention low-dose aspirin per USPSTF 2022 reduced indication (age 40-59 with 10-yr ASCVD ≥10% + low bleeding risk + shared decision-making; age ≥60 = against routine; secondary prevention always); this patient may qualify based on risk + shared decision-making; (5) GLUCOSE — HbA1c 6.0 (borderline) — lifestyle + monitoring + repeat 3-6 mo + initiate metformin if HbA1c ≥6.5 + symptomatic; (6) WAIST + WEIGHT management — abdominal obesity contributes to multimorbidity; gradual weight loss 5-10% body weight beneficial; (7) PHYSICAL ACTIVITY — 150+ min/week moderate intensity + 2 strength days; pulmonary rehab provides structured program; (8) DIET — Mediterranean-style + fruits + vegetables + whole grains + lean protein + healthy fats + limit ultra-processed + saturated fat + sodium; (9) STRESS management + sleep + mental health.
labs.r: COMPREHENSIVE COPD MANAGEMENT + PULMONARY REHAB + CARDIOVASCULAR RISK REDUCTION = multimorbidity care framework. Smoking cessation most important. Multimodal interventions. RN role: education + reinforcement + multidisciplinary coordination + chronic disease management.

labs.v: FOLLOW-UP plan for THIS PATIENT after smoking cessation initiation + comprehensive Health Promotion: (1) PHONE CHECK-IN at Day 7 (quit date) by RN/MA — confirm quit date + assess withdrawal + cravings + side effects of varenicline + reinforcement + emotional support; (2) IN-PERSON visit at WEEK 1 POST-QUIT (Day 14) — most important visit; assess success + difficulties + side effects + relapse risk + reinforce + adjust as needed; CO breath test to verify cessation if available; (3) WEEK 4 POST-QUIT — assess sustained quit + continued varenicline tolerance + behavioral progress; (4) MONTH 3 (end of 12-week varenicline course) — assess sustained cessation + decide on extension to 24 weeks if needed; (5) MONTH 6 + MONTH 12 — long-term follow-up + reinforcement + relapse prevention; (6) ANNUAL VISITS thereafter + lifelong support. RELAPSE PREVENTION strategies: (1) IDENTIFY high-risk situations (stress + alcohol + social settings with smokers + negative emotions + boredom + after meals + driving); (2) DEVELOP coping strategies + alternative behaviors; (3) BUILD social support network (family + friends + community + peer); (4) HANDLE slips without giving up — one cigarette doesn't equal failure + immediately recommit; (5) MAINTAIN connection with provider + Quitline + community; (6) RECOGNIZE that many quitters require 5-7 attempts before sustained cessation — normalize without judgment; (7) MEDICATION extension if relapse risk high; (8) REWARD success at milestones (1 day + 1 week + 1 month + 6 months + 1 year + 5 years + 10 years); (9) MEDICAL MILESTONES highlighted (improved breathing + cough decreasing + endurance + cardiovascular + cancer risk reductions over time). ONGOING CARE for COPD + cardiovascular + multimorbidity: PCP every 3-4 months stable + sooner if exacerbations; PULMONARY specialist annually + sooner if needed; CARDIOLOGY if cardiovascular concerns; ENDOCRINOLOGY if diabetes progression; SLEEP MEDICINE for OSA workup; PULMONARY REHAB completion + maintenance program; ADVANCE CARE PLANNING + advance directive discussion + POLST when appropriate; FAMILY involvement + community + support. SHARED DECISION-MAKING + autonomy + cultural sensitivity + health literacy throughout. INSURANCE coverage + Medicare/Medicaid + community resources + insurance navigation.
labs.r: FOLLOW-UP + ongoing care + relapse prevention = essential to sustained smoking cessation + comprehensive chronic disease management. RN role: phone check-ins + visits + reinforcement + monitoring + multidisciplinary coordination + lifelong support. Relapse common + normalize + recommit.

opts.text: DEFER smoking cessation pharmacotherapy until patient has had 6 months of continued failed attempts without medication; pharmacotherapy is reserved for refractory cases who have proven they cannot quit on their own + adds unnecessary cost + side effects

Pharmacotherapy DOUBLES quit success rates vs placebo + combined with behavioral counseling further increases. WAITING for failed attempts wastes opportunities + delays cessation + perpetuates harm. Standard practice = OFFER pharmacotherapy + behavioral counseling + shared decision-making at every quit attempt.

opts.text: Prescribe BUPROPION 150 mg PO BID concurrently with VARENICLINE 1 mg PO BID for maximal efficacy through dual mechanism; combination of nicotinic receptor partial agonist + dopamine/NE reuptake inhibitor should yield best results in this heavy smoker
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG combination. VARENICLINE + BUPROPION combination NOT supported by evidence (CHRONICUS trial showed no benefit + increased adverse effects). EFFECTIVE COMBINATIONS = varenicline + NRT OR bupropion + NRT OR patch + short-acting NRT. STANDARD: select one of three first-line agents (varenicline OR bupropion OR NRT) + combine with NRT (or different formulations of NRT) for heavy smokers.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What pharmacology + Health Promotion + behavioral principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: TOBACCO USE DISORDER leading preventable cause of death in US (~480,000 deaths/year); 5 AS FRAMEWORK (Ask + Advise + Assess + Assist + Arrange) per USPHS at every visit; STAGES OF CHANGE Model (precontemplation → contemplation → preparation → action → maintenance + relapse possible); MOTIVATIONAL INTERVIEWING (OARS) for not-yet-ready or ambivalent
opts.rat: CORRECT — foundational smoking cessation behavioral framework. Applied at every visit. RN role: implement + provide counseling + connect to resources.

opts.text: VARENICLINE PHARMACOLOGY = partial α4β2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist; MOST EFFECTIVE single agent (~25-30% quit rate vs ~10-15% placebo); 12-week titration with quit date 1-2 weeks after starting; NAUSEA most common AE; EAGLES trial 2016 removed previous black-box for neuropsychiatric AEs; CV concerns slight increase MACE but absolute risk low + benefits outweigh in stable CV; renal dose adjust CrCl <30
opts.rat: CORRECT — varenicline pharmacology + efficacy + safety framework. Evidence-based + first-line single agent. RN role: medication selection + education + monitoring.

opts.text: BUPROPION SR PHARMACOLOGY = atypical antidepressant + dopamine/NE reuptake inhibitor; 150 mg daily × 3 days → BID × 7-12 weeks; quit date 1 week after start; CONTRAINDICATIONS = SEIZURE DISORDER (absolute) + EATING DISORDER (absolute) + MAOI within 14 days + abrupt alcohol/benzo withdrawal + recent head trauma + severe hepatic; AE = insomnia (most common) + dry mouth + headache + tremor; black-box SI in <25 yr
opts.rat: CORRECT — bupropion pharmacology + contraindications + AE. Alternative when varenicline not appropriate + concurrent depression. RN role: selection + education + monitoring.

opts.text: NRT PHARMACOLOGY = nicotine replacement without combustion products; long-acting patch + short-acting gum/lozenge/inhaler/nasal spray; combination patch + short-acting more effective; dosing based on smoking intensity; CV concerns previously raised but evidence shows NRT safer than continued smoking in stable CV disease; can combine with varenicline or bupropion for heavy smokers
opts.rat: CORRECT — NRT framework + combinations. Foundation + alternative + combinable. RN role: dosing + administration + counseling.

opts.text: ACIP VACCINATIONS for adult with COPD + age 58: INFLUENZA annually + PCV20 (chronic lung disease) + TDAP every 10 yr + SHINGRIX 2-dose ≥50 yr + HBV catch-up universal 19-59 + COVID-19 per current; VIS + observation + documentation + registry; standard adult Health Promotion + chronic disease management
opts.rat: CORRECT — ACIP framework. Comprehensive adult immunization for chronic disease. RN role: review + administer + counsel + VIS + observation + registry.

opts.text: LDCT LUNG CANCER SCREENING USPSTF 2021 Grade B: age 50-80 + 20+ pack-years + currently smoking or quit within 15 yr; NLST + NELSON ~20% mortality reduction; SHARED DECISION-MAKING benefits + risks; Lung-RADS reporting + annual frequency; reinforce cessation
opts.rat: CORRECT — LDCT screening criteria + evidence + shared decision-making + reporting. Standard Health Promotion intervention. RN role: identify eligibility + counsel + order + track + reinforce cessation.

opts.text: PULMONARY REHAB Class A recommendation per GOLD + ATS for severe COPD + post-exacerbation; 8-12 week multidisciplinary; improves QOL + exercise capacity + dyspnea + reduces hospitalizations; SLEEP APNEA screening (STOP-BANG) high prevalence in COPD; CV RISK reduction (statin + ACE-I + lifestyle + ASA shared decision-making per USPSTF 2022); MULTIMORBIDITY chronic disease management
opts.rat: CORRECT — comprehensive COPD + multimorbidity care. Multidisciplinary + evidence-based. RN role: referrals + coordination + reinforcement + education.
